channel.title: DJ's Website – Software Engineering
channel.description: Recent content in Software Engineering on DJ's Website

item.title: Docs: big-O
item.description:
Flat is better than nested — The Zen of Python


How do you evaluate codes?

  • Time efficiency: Faster? Time complexity
  • Space efficiency: Less memory intensive?
  • More readable ?

Big O Notation

  • My summary
  • cheatsheet
  • n: the length of input to your function
  • Approximation: Worst, average, best case
  • Space efficiency: Copy code

What is big-O notation?

  • a fuzzy way for counting
  • a big picture of the time efficiency of the code

How to measure the code performance using big-O?

  • Assignment statements and if statements that are only executed once regardless of the size of the problem are $O(1)$.
  • A simple “for” loop from 0 to n (with no internal loops), contributes $O(n)$ (linear complexity); a nested loop of the same type (or bounded by the first loop parameter), gives $O(n^2)$ (quadratic complexity);
  • A loop in which the controlling parameter is divided by two at each step (and which terminates when it reaches 1), gives $O(log n)$ (logarithmic complexity);
  • A “while” loop may vary depends on the actual numbers of iterations it will run ( OK. It means as same as the for loop. That is to say, for all kinds of loops, we only care about the actual number of iterations that they’ve executed before they hit the upper bond).
  • A loop with a not-$O(1)$ execution inside, simply multiplies the complexity of the body of the loop, by the number of times the loop will execute.
  • When dealing with multiple statements, add them up.

Rules

  • Constants doesn’t matter

    • $O(2N) = O(N)$
    • $O(5) = O(500) = O(1)$

  • Smaller terms doesn’t matter

    • $O(N^2 +50N +10) == O(N^2)$

  • $O(1) < O(log n) < O(Sqrt(n) ) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n^2) < O(n^3) < O(2^n)$

What are the big-O notations from these ML techniques?

  • Linear: $O(N)$
  • Sorting: $O(nlogN)$

BigO of various operations in current CPython

Time Complexity


Searching and Sorting

Binary Search

  • Definition
  • Efficiency: $O(log(n))$, not $O(N)$, typically base 2 in CS

Bubble Sort

  • Time complexity: (n-1)(n-1): $n^2$
  • Space complexity: Auxillary space O(1) because we don’t need extra arrays etc . In space sorting algorithm

Merge Sort

  • Divide and Conquer
    • Break the array down into small pieces and build it up along the way
  • time complexity: $nlog(n)$
  • space complexity: $O(n)$, need to copy array at each step

Quick Sort

  • Pivot: pick up a random value
    • But the convention is to pick the last one as a pivot
  • Method: move the pivot in the middle
    • run from both directions
    • select the median of the last few pivots
  • Time complexity:
    • Worst case: $O(n^2)$, the biggest numbers are already in the right place (e.g. end)
    • Average case: $nlog(n)$

Selection Sort


References

  • Everything about big-o
  • SVM Analysis
  • RF Analysis
  • Complete Beginner’s Guide to Big ONotation
  • Big O Notation
  • You (Probably) Don’t Need For-Loops
  • Neural networks and Big(O)
  • Back Prop
  • Big-O cheatsheet

item.title: Docs: Data Structure
item.description:
Data types

  • Simple types
boolean = True
number = 1.1
string = "Strings can be declared with single or double quotes."
list = ["Lists can have", 1, 2, 3, 4, "or more types together!"]
tuple = ("Tuples", "can have", "more than", 2, "elements!")
dictionary = {'one': 1, 'two': 2, 'three': 3}
variable_with_zero_data = None

Dynamic Sets

  • Sets Fundamental to computer science as they are to mathematics. Whereas mathematical sets are unchanging, the sets manipulated by algorithms can grow, shrink, or otherwise change over time. We call such sets dynamic.

  • Dictionary We call a dynamic set that supports these operations (the ability to insert elements into, delete elements from, and test membership in a set)

Operations on dynamic sets

  • Queries: return information about the est
    • Search(S, k): Search S using key value k
    • minimum
    • maxium
    • Successor(S,x): given an element x, whose key is from a totally ordered set S, returns a pointer to the next larger element in S, or null if x is the maxium
    • Predecessor(S,x): given an element x, whose key is from a totally ordered set S, returns a pointer to the next smaller element in S, or null if x is the maxium
  • Modifying operations: which change the set
    • insert
    • delete

Array

  • index
  • insertion and deletion O(n) in the worst case

Behind the scenes a Python list is built as an array. Even though you can do many operations on a Python list with just one line of code, there’s a lot of code built in to the Python language running to make that operation possible.

For example, inserting into a list is easy (happens in constant time). However, inserting into an array is O(n), since you may need to shift elements to make space for the one you’re inserting, or even copy everything to a new array if you run out of space. Thus, inserting into a Python list is actually O(n), while operations that search for an element at a particular spot are O(1). You can see the runtime of other list operations here


List

Linked lists

  • An array’s linear order is determined by the array indices
  • A linked list is a data structure in which the objects are arranged in a linear order
  • Searching in a linked list
  • LIST-DELETE runs in O.1/ time, but if we wish to delete an element with a given key, ‚.n/ time is required in the worst case because we must first call LIST-SEARCH to find the element.


Image from Medium


Circular Linked List


Array vs List

  • Array: like a set of boxes, where each has an address called an index
  • Linked list: an extension of a list, like a paper chain, characterized by links, the one before and the one after. Not knowing where it is
    • There are no indices
    • There are orders
  • Adding and removing elements from arrays are complicated O(N)
    Arrays vs List
  • Complexity of adding and deleting an element
    • Array: O(N)
    • Linked List: Constant O(1)
      • Change the next reference to a new object

Similarities

  • Both store data: e.g. a value of 8

Information storage

  • Array: stores indices
  • Linked list: stores a “next” component
    • Which stores the reference to the next element in the list. It looks like assigning the next element as a property to the current element.
    • Way down at the hardware level, your element has some space dedicated for it in memory. So the “next” component represents the memory address of the next element

Lists vs Arrays vs Sets vs Maps

  • Lists
    • Linked List: easy
    • Dynamic linked list
  • Array: list based structure
  • Sets: Like a bag, a box of chocolate, you don’t know what you are gonna get
  • Maps: set-based structure, a group of keys is a set
    • In Python, the map concept appears as a built-in data type called a dictionary. A dictionary contains key-value pairs.

Stacks

  • LIFO policy: last in, first out
    • The element deleted from the set is the one most recently inserted
  • A stack of chips in pringles can
  • When you only care the most recent elements (new changes)
    • Push: O(1), most recently inserted elements
    • Pop: O(1), delete operation (does not take an element argument)
  • Abstract
  • Using other data types as stacks
    • e.g.list
  • Underflows: pop an empty stack
  • Overflows: if S.top >n

Queues

  • FIFO policy: first in, first out
  • A queue of people at the cashier in the supermarket
  • Head: the first element
  • Tail: the newest
  • Enqueue: add an element to the tail
  • Dequeue: remove the head element
  • Peek: look at the head element but do not remove it
  • Two types of Queues
    • Dequeue: a generalized version of stacks and queues
    • Priority Queue
      • Assign each element a priority
      • Dequeue: remove the element with the highest priority

Hash

Why?

  • Without hash: looking up in sets or lists is linear time
  • With hash function in data structure: constant time lookups

Hash Functions

  • Based on array’s index

  • why? To transform some values to hash values which can be stored and looked up easily

  • Why constant time lookup works?

    • You give your number to a hash function, which spits out a hash code that turns into the index of an array
    • You can go to your array and get your original value in constant time
    • since an array look up with an index happens in constant time

  • E.g. assign the last few digits to a hash functions, because the last few digits are the most random

  • Use the remainder as a key

  • Collisions: the hash function spits out the same index value for two different input values

    • change hash functions by using a big number (e.g. five last few digits, instead of one)
      • Take a lot of space
      • More time and space complexity if changing hash functions every time the array changes
    • Bucket: list
      • less space
      • may increase time complexity

  • Load factor:

    Load Factor = Number of Entries / Number of Buckets For example, if we’re trying to store 10 values in a hash table with 1000 buckets, the load factor would be 0.01, and the majority of buckets in the table will be empty. We end up wasting memory by having so many empty buckets, so we may want to rehash, or come up with a new hash function with less buckets. We can use our load factor as an indicator for when to rehash—as the load factor approaches 0, the more empty, or sparse, our hash table is. On the flip side, the closer our load factor is to 1 (meaning the number of values equals the number of buckets), the better it would be for us to rehash and add more buckets. Any table with a load value greater than 1 is guaranteed to have collisions.

What is the upside and downside of hashing?

String hashing

In this quiz, you’ll write your own hash table and hash function that uses string keys. Your table will store strings in buckets by their first two letters, according to the formula below:

Hash Value = (ASCII Value of First Letter * 100) + ASCII Value of Second Letter

  • You can assume that the string will have at least two letters, and the first two characters are uppercase letters (ASCII values from 65 to 90).
  • You can use the Python function ord() to get the ASCII value of a letter, and chr() to get the letter associated with an ASCII value.
  • You’ll create a HashTable class, methods to store and lookup values, and a helper function to calculate a hash value given a string.
  • You cannot use a Python dictionary—only lists!
  • And remember to store lists at each bucket, and not just the string itself.
  • For example, you can store “UDACITY” at index 8568 as [“UDACITY”].
class HashTable(object):
    def __init__(self):
        self.table = [None]*10000

    def store(self, string):
        hv = self.calculate_hash_value(string)
        if hv != -1:
            if self.table[hv] != None:
                self.table[hv].append(string)
            else:
                self.table[hv] = [string]

    def lookup(self, string):
        hv = self.calculate_hash_value(string)
        if hv != -1:
            if self.table[hv] != None:
                if string in self.table[hv]:
                    return hv
        return -1

    def calculate_hash_value(self, string):
        value = ord(string[0])*100 + ord(string[1])
        return value
  • 31 is used by convention

Tree

  • Similar to linked list
  • It’s drawn vertically, instead of horizontally
  • A tree must be completely connected
  • A tree does not have cycles
  • Terms
    • Level
      • Root: Level 1
      • Parent: Ancestor: lower level
      • Child: Descendant: higher level
      • Leaf or external node : a node without children nodes
      • Height of a node: the number of edges between it and the furthest leaf on the tree
      • Height (overall): the hight of the root node
      • Depth: the number of edges to the root
      • Height and depth move inversely

Binary Tree

  • Parents have at most two children (0,1,2 children)
  • Search: O(n)
  • Delete: O(n)
  • Insert: find a open spot
    • Worst: O(n)
# https://classroom.udacity.com/courses/ud513/lessons/7122604912/concepts/79211181170923
class BinaryTree(object):
    def __init__(self, root):
        self.root = Node(root)

    def search(self, find_val):
        return self.preorder_search(tree.root, find_val)

    def print_tree(self):
        return self.preorder_print(tree.root, "")[:-1]

    def preorder_search(self, start, find_val):
        if start:
            if start.value == find_val:
                return True
            else:
                return self.preorder_search(start.left, find_val) or self.preorder_search(start.right, find_val)
        return False

    def preorder_print(self, start, traversal):
        if start:
            traversal += (str(start.value) + "-")
            traversal = self.preorder_print(start.left, traversal)
            traversal = self.preorder_print(start.right, traversal)
        return traversal

Tree Traversal

  • DFS: Depth-first search

    • Convention: left to right
    • If there are children nodes to explore, explore them is definitely the priority
    • Pre-Order
    • In-Order
    • Post-Order

  • BFS: Breadth-first search

  • Explore nodes on the same level is the priority (before visiting child nodes)
  • A level order traversal
  • Convention: left to right
  • Wost case: O(n), skewed
class BST(object):
    def __init__(self, root):
        self.root = Node(root)

    def insert(self, new_val):
        self.insert_helper(self.root, new_val)

    def insert_helper(self, current, new_val):
        if current.value < new_val:
            if current.right:
                self.insert_helper(current.right, new_val)
            else:
                current.right = Node(new_val)
        else:
            if current.left:
                self.insert_helper(current.left, new_val)
            else:
                current.left = Node(new_val)

    def search(self, find_val):
        return self.search_helper(self.root, find_val)

    def search_helper(self, current, find_val):
        if current:
            if current.value == find_val:
                return True
            elif current.value < find_val:
                return self.search_helper(current.right, find_val)
            else:
                return self.search_helper(current.left, find_val)
        return False

Self-Balancing Tree

  • Unbalanced tree: a linked list (extreme imbalanced)
  • Binary search trees:
    • Ordered, or sorted, binary trees
    • Nodes can have 2 sub trees
    • Items to the left of a given node are smaller
  • Red-Black Tree: assure the tree never get too imbalanced
    • Rule 1: A node is either red or black
    • Rule 2: The root and leaves (NIL nodes) are black
    • Rule 3: If a node is red, then it’s children are black
    • Rule 4: All paths from a node to its NIL descendants contain the same number of black nodes
      • We don’t count the starting node
      • We can’t the nil node
    • Search: O(log(n))
    • Insert: O(log(n))
    • Remote: O(log(n))
  • Rotation to keep red-black and BST

Heaps

  • A type of trees with specific rules
  • In a heap, elements are arranged in increasing or decreasing order, such that the root element is either the maximum or minimum value in the tree
  • Two different heaps
    • Max heaps: Parents must always have bigger values than its children
    • Min heaps: Parents have lower values than its children
  • Heaps don’t need to be binary trees
  • A binary max heap:
  • All level except the last level must be full
  • The function that gets the maximal value, Peak, is O(1), the root
  • Worst case: O(n)
  • Heapify: the function to reorder the tree based on the heap property: O(log(n)) as the height of the tree

Heap Implementation

  • Store as an array: save space
  • Store as array: value and pointers (left child, right child, parent)

Graph

  • Connectivity: the minimal number of edge to be removed

  • Disconnected Disconnected graphs are very similar whether the graph’s directed or undirected—there is some vertex or group of vertices that have no connection with the rest of the graph.

  • Weakly Connected A directed graph is weakly connected when only replacing all of the directed edges with undirected edges can cause it to be connected. Imagine that your graph has several vertices with one outbound edge, meaning an edge that points from it to some other vertex in the graph. There’s no way to reach all of those vertices from any other vertex in the graph, but if those edges were changed to be undirected all vertices would be easily accessible.

  • Connected Here we only use “connected graph” to refer to undirected graphs. In a connected graph, there is some path between one vertex and every other vertex.

Strongly Connected Strongly connected directed graphs must have a path from every node and every other node. So, there must be a path from A to B

Cycle in Graph

  • DAG: Directed Acyclic Graph: Directed graph without cycles
  • Cycles can be dangerous, infinite loops

Graph Representations

  • Vertex object
  • Edge object
  • Edge list: 2D list
  • Adjacency list

Ref

  • Intro to Data Structures and Algorithms
  • MIT
  • khanacademy

item.title: Docs: Efficiency
item.description:
Efficiency

  • Space: the amount of memory
  • Time

Big O Notation: O(n)

  • My summary
  • cheatsheet
  • n: the length of input to your function
  • Approximation: Worst, average, best case
  • Space efficiency: Copy code

Searching and Sorting

Binary search

  • Definition
  • Efficiency: $O(log(n))$, not O(N), typically base 2 in CS

Complexity of adding and deleting an element

  • Array: O(N)
  • Linked List: Constant O(1)
    • Change the next reference to a new object

Bubble sort

  • Time complexity: (n-1)(n-1): $n^2$
  • Space complexity: Auxillary space O(1) because we don’t need extra arrays etc . In space sorting algorithm

Merge sort

  • Divide and Conquer
    • Break the array down into small pieces and build it up along the way
  • time complexity: $nlog(n$
  • space complexity: O(n), need to copy array at each step

Quick Sort

  • Pivot: pick up a random value
    • But the convention is to pick the last one as a pivot
  • Method: move the pivot in the middle
    • run from both directions
    • select the median of the last few pivots
  • Time complexity:
    • Worst case: $O(n^2)$, the biggest numbers are already in the right place (e.g. end)
    • Average case: $nlog(n)$

Selection Sort

LoL


Image from xkcd

item.title: Docs: Memory
item.description:
Memory

  • Address
    • Ampersand &: address operator, tells what address is
    • The asterisk “*": dereference, go to the address
  • Pointers
    • like an arrow pointing to a physical address in the memory
    • In computer science, a pointer is an object in many programming languages that stores a memory address. This can be that of another value located in computer memory, or in some cases, that of memory-mapped computer hardware.
    • A pointer references a location in memory, and obtaining the value stored at that location is known as dereferencing the pointer.
    • pointer
    • 8 bytes in modern computer systems

Memory layout

A big chunk that treats different programs

  • Machine code: zeros and ones of your programs
  • Global function
  • heap: a special chunk of function every time you call malloc
    • malloc (memory allocation)
    • allocate memory on the heap type
  • stack: call a function, use stack function, swap, sting compare
    • a dynamic space
  • garbage values
  • NULL: for pointers, no address
  • \0: for character
  • free memory: free(), the opposite of malloc
  • malloc
    • how much memory do you want?
    • stdio.h

Memory: string

  • string: one byte away
  • string exists as char *s :
    • array of characters
    • the address of the first character
  • sting: just the pointer of address in memory t=s t is pointing the address
  • copy string

Hexadecimal

  • Decimal
  • Hexadecimal: 16 digits
    • 0 to 9
    • A to F (10-15)
    • base 16:
      • $16^0 to 16^9$
    • 0x: indicates hexadecimal code
    • RGB
      • 000000: no color, black
      • FFFFFF: white, a lot of RGB
      • FF0000: Red
      • 00FF00: Green
      • 0000FF: Blue
    • FF: 255
    • One hexadecimal digit is equal to 4 binary digits

What is 32-bit, what is 64-bit?

  • A 32-bit system can access 232 memory addresses, i.e 4 GB of RAM or physical memory ideally, it can access more than 4 GB of RAM also.
  • A 64-bit system can access 264 memory addresses, i.e actually 18-Quintillion bytes of RAM. In short, any amount of memory greater than 4 GB can be easily handled by it.

Memory Management

Basics

  • Fixed partition
    • Partition memory table
    • Static
  • Dynamic partition available memory
    • Jobs are only given as much memory as they require
  • De-allocation: when a job is finished, it is removed from partition

Methods

  • first fit allocation: find the first partition that has sufficient space
  • best fit: looks for the smallest partition, resulting more partition for other jobs
    • slower performance
    • but better efficiency

Memory Management Unit (MMU)

  • The hardware responsible for implementing virtual memory
  • Sit between the CPU core and memory
    • Most often part of the physical CPU
    • Separate from the RAM controller
  • Function: Handles all memory access from Load/Store instructions
  • Translation look aside Buffer

Stack Overflow

  • the memory in stacks runs over the heap, touch memories that it shouldn’t
  • invocation
  • recursion
  • cyclical danger
    • segmentation
    • merge sort

Buffer Overflow

  • Buffer: a chunk of memory
  • Buffering: a buffer is retrieved by malloc is allocating
  • Buffer overflow

Enhance

  • reduce pixelation
  • use ML and find patterns that are not visible to human eyes
  • fread
  • fprintf: print to file
  • file has 3 digits

Memory debugs

  • valgrind: a program that detects memory debugs
    • if you forget to call free
      • a memory leak: a program that keeps allocating memory
  • binky

How to free memory in python?

import gc
gc.collect()

#or 
del

Memory allocation

Page Faults

A CPU exception, generated when software attempts to use an invalid virtual address

  • Virtual address is not mapped
  • The processes has insufficient permissions for the address
  • The virtual address is valid, but swapped out

Kernel Virtual Memory

  • Kernel logical address
  • Logical memories can not be swapped out

Virtual Memory

  • a virtual mapping
  • Restriction to allocation
  • Each app is virtua
  • Paged allocation memory
    • Sectors
    • Page frames
  • Translation lookup battle (TLB)

Ref

  • CS50 2020 - Lecture 4 - Memory (pre-release)
  • Introduction to Memory Management in Linux

item.title: Docs: Testing
item.description:
Testing

Defensive programming

Testing Basics

  • Eliminating syntax errors and static semantic errors
    • python can do
  • Test cases
    • input
    • output
  • Three general classes
    • Unit Testing: each function runs according to the specification
    • Regression testing
      • add test for bugs as you find them
      • catch reintroduced errors that were previously fixed
    • Integration testing: after the first two cycles, make sure the interaction between pieces work together

How to test

  • Test by my own
  • Write a program to do test, e.e.g test.py
# ./test0.sh
python3 -c "from test0 import test_prime; test_prime(25,False)"
python3 -c "from test0 import test_prime; test_prime(23,True)"

Testing Methods

  • Intuition about natural boundaries to the problem
  • If no natural partitions
    • might do random testing, the more testing, the more accurate
    • black box testing
      • explore paths through specification only
    • glass box testing
      • explore paths through code
      • use code to directly guide design of testing cases
      • path complete: if every potential path through code is tested at least once
      • drawbacks:
      • can go through loops arbitrarily many times
      • missing paths
      • guidelines:
      • branches: exercises all parts of a conditional
      • for loops:
        • loop not entered
        • body of loop executed exactly once
        • body of loop executed more than once
      • while loops
        • same as for loops
        • cases that catch all ways to exit loop

What is a path-complete test suite?

A test set that covers all paths

Is a path complete test suite good enough?

  • No.
  • A path complete test suite could still miss a bug
  • Should still hit boundary cases (0, -1)

Assert

  • at the beginning of a function
  • assert what you expects, if the assertion is false, what to do and the function terminates
  • prevents the program propagating bad values, getting an unexpected results at the end

When to use assertions?

  • raise exceptions if users supply bad data input
  • use assertions to
    • check types of arguments of values
    • check that invariants on data structures are met
    • check constraints on return values
    • check for violations of constraints on procedure (e.g. no duplicates)

Debugging

History of debugging

Mark II, Debugging of a real bug

Debugging tools

  • anaconda
  • python tutor
  • print

How to systematically debug?

  • Inside functions (loop, correct values)
  • Bisectional methods (first half, second half)
  • Don’t test or debug entire program
  • Backup code (make a copy and git commit)

Debugging methods

  • Remember common error messages
  • Logic messages
    • Go back to the basics (Ruber ducky or someone who doesn’t understand anything)

Use Handler

  • Try and except block: the handler handles the error message, makes a lot easier to read
    • you can also catch specific errors: like if else error capture
  • Else block: body of this block is executed when execution of associated try body completes with no exceptions
  • Final block: always executed
  • False silently:
    • just substitute default values or just continue
    • bad idea. users get no warning
  • Return an “error” value
    • what value to choose?
    • complicates code having to check for a special value (a lot of senarios)
  • Stop execution, signal error condition
    • Raising your own exceptions, flag the error message
      • print exceptions
    • Change Policy,
      • except “Error”, return 0

Ref

  • Testing methods
